content:
# Joshua Chapter 16 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), page 240 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_016_page_001.jpg`

---

1 And the lot went out for the sons of Yosef from the Yarden [at] Yericho, to the waters of Yericho eastward, the wilderness going up from Yericho through the hill country [of] Beyth-El.

2 And it went out from Beyth-El to Luz, and passed over to the border of the Arki to Ataroth.

3 And it went down westward to the border of the Yaphleti, unto the border of Beyth-Choron the lower, and unto Gezer; and its goings out were to the sea.

4 And the sons of Yosef — Menasheh and Ephrayim — received [their] inheritance.

5 And the border of the sons of Ephrayim according to their clans was: and the border of their inheritance eastward [was] Ataroth-Addar, unto Beyth-Choron the upper.

6 And the border went out westward [at] Mikhmethath on the north, and the border turned eastward [to] Ta'anath-Shiloh, and passed over it eastward [to] Yanoach.

7 And it went down from Yanoach [to] Ataroth and [to] Na'arah, and reached Yericho, and went out at the Yarden.

8 From Tappuach the border went westward to the wadi of Qanah, and its goings out were to the sea. This [is] the inheritance of the tribe of the sons of Ephrayim according to their clans.

9 And the cities that were set apart for the sons of Ephrayim [were] in the midst of the inheritance of the sons of Menasheh — all the cities and their villages.

10 And they did not drive out the Kena'ani dwelling in Gezer; and the Kena'ani dwelt in the midst of Ephrayim unto this day, and became [laborers of] forced labor.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v1 — הַגּוֹרָל (haggoral):** "The lot" — the territorial allocation for the sons of Yosef (Joseph) was determined by sacred lot (גּוֹרָל), the same method described in Josh 14:2. This chapter describes the combined Josephite boundary before specifying Ephraim's portion in vv. 5–9.

2. **v2 — Beyth-El to Luz:** The MT reads מִבֵּית־אֵל לוּזָה, "from Beyth-El to Luz." This is unusual because Gen 28:19 identifies Beyth-El and Luz as the same place. The Masoretic distinction may indicate two closely adjacent settlements or an older and newer settlement site. The text is preserved without harmonization.

3. **v3 — הַיַּפְלֵטִי (haYaphleti):** "The Yaphleti" — a clan or family group whose territory formed part of the southern boundary. The gentilic form with the definite article is retained.

4. **v3 — יָמָּה (yammah):** Literally "seaward," rendered "westward" throughout this chapter, as the Mediterranean Sea defined the western orientation in Hebrew geographic reckoning. The same term appears in vv. 6 and 8.

5. **v4 — וַיִּנְחֲלוּ (wayyinchalu):** "Received [their] inheritance" — the Qal wayyiqtol of נחל, indicating the act of taking possession by inheritance. The verse serves as a summary heading: both Menasheh and Ephrayim together constitute the "sons of Yosef."

6. **v5 — Boundary description complexity:** The Hebrew of v. 5 is notably compressed and syntactically difficult. The phrase וַיְהִי גְּבוּל בְּנֵי־אֶפְרַיִם לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם ("And the border of the sons of Ephrayim according to their clans was") opens with a casus pendens construction, and the precise delineation of the eastern boundary is abbreviated compared to the full circuit given for other tribes.

7. **v6 — הַמִּכְמְתָת (hammikhmethath):** This place name appears also in Josh 17:7. Its location is uncertain, generally placed near Shechem. The definite article suggests it may originally have been a descriptive term ("the hiding-place" from כמת/כמן) that became a proper noun.

8. **v8 — נַחַל קָנָה (nachal Qanah):** "The wadi of Qanah" — literally "the reed wadi." This seasonal watercourse formed the boundary between Ephrayim and Menasheh (cf. Josh 17:9). The word קָנָה (reed) is the same root from which the English "cane" ultimately derives via Greek κάννα.

9. **v9 — Separated cities:** The Hebrew הַמִּבְדָּלוֹת ("that were set apart") indicates that certain cities within Menasheh's territorial block were allocated to Ephrayim. This interlocking of tribal territories was a distinctive feature of the Josephite allotment, reflecting perhaps their shared ancestry and the practical realities of settlement.

10. **v10 — וְלֹא הוֹרִישׁוּ (welo horishu):** "And they did not drive out" — the Hiphil of ירשׁ, meaning to dispossess or drive out. This notice of failure to expel the Kena'ani parallels Judg 1:29 and forms part of the recurring refrain of incomplete conquest (cf. Josh 15:63, 17:12–13). The Kena'ani are reduced to מַס (mas), "forced labor" or corvée, rather than being expelled — a compromise that the book of Judges will later present as a source of ongoing trouble.

11. **v10 — עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה (ad hayyom hazzeh):** "Unto this day" — this formulaic phrase indicates the situation persisted at the time of the text's composition or final editing. It serves as an editorial marker anchoring the narrative to the writer's present.

12. **Place name transliterations:** All place names are transliterated from the Hebrew rather than Anglicized: Beyth-El (בֵּית־אֵל, "House of God"), Beyth-Choron (בֵּית חוֹרֹן, "House of the Hollow"), Gezer (גֶּזֶר), Tappuach (תַּפּוּחַ, "Apple"), Yanoach (יָנוֹחַ, "Rest"), Na'arah (נַעֲרָה). These preserve the meaningful Hebrew roots embedded in the toponymy of the land.
